title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
short_title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people to get extra time to file their taxes when disasters happen. Right now, the government only gives tax deadline extensions when the federal government declares a disaster. This new law would let state governors ask for tax deadline extensions even when only the state declares a disaster, like from floods, fires, or other natural disasters. The bill also increases the automatic extension time from 60 days to 120 days for people who live or work in disaster areas or who help with disaster relief.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Modification of Rules for Postponing Certain Deadlines by Reason of Disaster
sections.text: This section changes the tax rules about delaying deadlines when disasters happen. First, it gives the Treasury Secretary new power to delay federal tax deadlines when a state governor or the mayor of Washington DC declares a disaster. The governor or mayor must write a letter asking for this help. The disaster must be a natural event like a hurricane, tornado, storm, flood, high water, tidal wave, tsunami, earthquake, volcano eruption, landslide, mudslide, snowstorm, or drought. It can also be a fire, flood, or explosion from any cause. The governor or mayor must decide that the damage is serious enough to need tax deadline relief. The word state in this law includes Washington DC, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. Second, this section changes how long certain tax deadline extensions must last. Instead of being required to give at least sixty days, the Treasury Secretary must now give at least one hundred twenty days. These changes will only apply to disaster declarations made after this law is signed.
